_comment: Query-string external bake-off — 'should I use kitsoki for my project?'. Real live results: GPT-5.5 (codex-native) solved 3/3 real query-string bugs through the kitsoki bugfix pipeline, graded against each PR's own regression test as a hidden oracle. GLM-5.2 (synthetic) is marked pending — the synthetic subscription was rate-limited at run time (verified by direct probe), NOT a capability result. Scene types match stories/slidey-edit/schemas/deck.json so it bundles to static HTML via `slidey bundle`. Numbers are real, from tools/bugfix-bakeoff/external/results/*.json; nothing invented.

meta.title: Should I use kitsoki for my project?

scenes.title: Should I use kitsoki for my project?
scenes.subtitle: Onboard a real repo — then let a model fix real bugs through the pipeline
scenes.narration: We point the kitsoki bug-fix pipeline at a real third-party repo, onboard it, and let a non-Claude model fix real filed-issue bugs — grading every fix against the regression test the real pull request shipped.

scenes.body: A prospective user asks one thing: if I onboard my repository and let kitsoki fix a bug, do I get a GOOD fix — and what does it cost versus the fix the maintainers actually shipped? To answer honestly we need a real, mature codebase and real bugs with a deterministic right answer.
scenes.narration: The question a prospective user actually asks: if I onboard my repo and let kitsoki fix a bug, do I get a good fix, and what does it cost versus the real one. To answer that honestly we need a real codebase and real bugs with a deterministic right answer.

scenes.title: The setup
scenes.narration: We onboard sindresorhus slash query-string — small and simple, one parser, but mature: two hundred seventy-four commits and ninety releases. Three real filed-issue bugs, each graded against the regression test its real pull request added, kept hidden from the model.

cards.label: 3 real bugs
cards.sub: filed issues → fixing PRs w/ tests

cards.label: hidden oracle
cards.sub: the PR's own regression test = ground truth

scenes.title: The 3 bugs — real issues, real fixes

cells: encoded separator %7C splits a single value
types schema ignored, comma + single item

scenes.caption: Each baseline is the real fix commit's parent — the bug is present, the checkout is byte-reproducible, and the fix's own added test is the hidden grader.

scenes.body: We onboard the repo with the embedded dev-story (config + instance + studio MCP + skill/agent toolkit), then drive the seven-room bugfix pipeline LIVE — the worker model reproduces, proposes, implements, tests, reviews and validates. The fix is graded with no LLM: overlay the hidden oracle, run it. GREEN = the bug is gone; RED = it isn't. The full suite is a second signal.
scenes.narration: We onboard the repo with the embedded dev-story, then drive the seven-room bug-fix pipeline live. The fix is then graded with no model in the loop: overlay the hidden regression test and run it. Green means the bug is gone.

scenes.title: GPT-5.5 through the kitsoki pipeline — 3 / 3 solved

cells: coerce single comma value to array (1452aa7, 12+/1−)

scenes.caption: Every fix passes the hidden oracle AND the full AVA suite. GPT-5.5 did all the work inside the session; the orchestrator only clicked the pipeline forward.

scenes.lede: A good fix updates the tests it breaks.
scenes.body: For qs1 and qs2, a correct behavioural fix legitimately flips a PRE-EXISTING test whose expectation encoded the bug — the real maintainer PR edited that test too. A model that only patches base.js leaves the suite RED (partial). GPT-5.5 driven through the pipeline ran the full suite, saw the break, and updated the affected test — landing all three at suite-green 'solved'. That's the 'kitsoki does more tests' lever, measured rather than asserted.
scenes.narration: Here's the quality lever. For two of the bugs, a correct fix legitimately breaks a pre-existing test — the real pull request updated it too. A model that only patches the source leaves the suite red. Through the pipeline, GPT-5.5 ran the full suite, caught the break, and updated the test — all three landed at suite-green solved.

scenes.title: kitsoki's fix vs the real maintainer fix

cells: same: split only on literal separators
force array for single comma value (3e61882)
same: coerce single value to typed array
same: decode then split on separator

scenes.caption: Not just oracle-green — GPT-5.5 converged on the SAME root-cause fix the maintainers shipped, in all three cases.

scenes.title: Cost — the honest accounting
scenes.caption: Codex traces carry no per-call price (ChatGPT-subscription auth); metered providers expose authoritative cost in the kitsoki trace (payload.meta.cost_usd). GLM-5.2's cells are pending — the synthetic subscription was rate-limited (verified by direct probe), so its result is withheld rather than reported as a capability finding.

scenes.lede: A reusable benchmark + a fixed delegation primitive.
scenes.body: Two durable artifacts fell out of this study. First, the bake-off is now a repo-agnostic benchmark: drop a projects/<name>/manifest.yaml and bench.py grades any repo against its own PR regression tests — a way to evaluate kitsoki's prompts and patterns on real code. Second, the headless MCP delegation pattern is fixed: a raw `claude -p --mcp-config --strict-mcp-config` attaches the studio MCP (an in-process subagent can't), encapsulated in tools/mcp-drive/drive.sh — the live worker model is chosen per session by the harness profile.
scenes.narration: Two durable artifacts fell out of this. The bake-off is now a generic benchmark — drop a manifest and grade any repo against its own pull-request tests. And the headless MCP delegation pattern is fixed: a raw claude print with an explicit mcp-config attaches the studio, which an in-process subagent cannot.

scenes.lede: Onboard your repo. Fix real bugs. Grade them honestly.
scenes.body: Pointed at a real, mature codebase, the kitsoki pipeline took GPT-5.5 to 3-for-3 on real filed-issue bugs — each fix matching the maintainer's own approach and passing the full test suite. Reproduce it with `make qs-bakeoff` (deterministic, free), then run your own cost-bearing cells through tools/mcp-drive. The GLM-5.2 comparison lands once the provider throttle clears.
scenes.narration: Pointed at a real mature codebase, the pipeline took GPT-5.5 to three for three on real bugs — each matching the maintainer's own approach and passing the full suite. Reproduce it with make qs-bakeoff, then run your own cells. The GLM comparison lands once the provider throttle clears.
